full_text: <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.56|> May these words of my mouth and this meditation of all of our hearts be pleasing in your sight,<|5.92|> <|6.54|> Lord our rock and our redeemer.<|7.98|> <|8.64|> Amen.<|9.02|> <|14.30|> So you know that classic dream where you show up at school and you've forgotten you have an exam?<|20.08|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|1.20|> LAUGHTER<|1.72|><|endoftext|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> I don't have that dream anymore, but what instills that same level of panic in me on Sunday mornings<|7.00|> <|7.00|> is when I'm really enjoying one of the hymns like the last one we sang,<|12.06|> <|12.70|> and I think, what if my microphone is on?<|14.98|> <|17.66|> That is not a sound any of you want to hear.<|21.14|> <|21.96|> There's a reason I always speak the Eucharist.<|25.80|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> I had that moment. It was like instant sweat.<|3.26|> <|7.40|> When our children were young, their favorite bedtime stories came from several volumes of read aloud Bible stories written and illustrated by a woman named Ella K. Lindvall.<|23.60|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> They wanted stories from these books every single night before bed.<|6.08|> <|6.52|> They loved them so much, in fact, that I have given them as gifts to all of my great nieces and nephews over the years.<|17.60|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> They're wonderful books with tight prose and descriptive words, many onomatopoetic, which<|7.44|> <|7.44|> I particularly love.<|8.58|> <|9.16|> Beautiful, simple, vibrant illustrations.<|11.98|> <|13.46|> This story from Mark, this story of Jesus falling asleep in the back of the boat and<|20.28|> <|20.28|> then calming the storm, was always one of their favorites.<|24.36|> <|24.36|> It's a great story.<|24.90|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> The story had all the great sounds of the wind wailing.<|5.14|> <|5.84|> Ooh, I liked doing that part.<|8.34|> <|9.38|> And the waves slap, slap, slapping against the hull of the boat.<|14.36|> <|15.32|> And as the reader, you would naturally rise to this dramatic crescendo, or at least I would,<|22.40|> <|23.10|> with the disciples panicking and begging Jesus to awake.<|27.94|> <|27.94|> Okay.<|28.90|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> And then you'd finish off the story, or I would, with that stage whisper of who then is this that even the wind and the sea obey him?<|17.60|><|endoftext|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> It's great stuff.<|2.60|> <|3.80|> I miss reading aloud at bedtime, and this story has all of the elements of a great end-of-the-day tale.<|11.98|> <|14.24|> Do you recall back in Advent when we began our journey with Mark?<|20.64|> <|20.64|> Mark.<|21.00|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> I shared with you some of the hallmarks of this particular gospel, some things to watch for throughout the course of the year.<|6.84|> <|7.42|> And one was that Mark, of all the gospel writers, does not think very highly of the disciples.<|15.62|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> And this text makes that point abundantly clear.<|4.34|> <|4.74|> The disciples have been with Jesus through his performance of miracles.<|10.68|> <|11.54|> He's cleansed a leper. He's healed the withering hand.<|15.26|> <|15.68|> He's cast out demons. He's taught in the synagogue and on the shores of the sea.<|22.70|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> He's taught people through parables, and he's been followed, not just by the disciples, but by great crowds.<|10.24|> <|11.48|> The disciples accompanied Jesus through all of this, but they still seem to fail to really get who he is.<|24.00|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> It has been pointed out that we should go a little easy on them and not dismiss the severity of this storm.<|7.76|> <|8.46|> It's good to remember that these were, after all, a bunch of fishermen who made their life on these very waters<|17.10|> <|17.10|> and had likely endured many a strong storm, encounters with terrible weather and rough seas,<|24.98|> <|24.98|> and they probably did not scare easily.<|29.10|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> But they are scared.<|2.84|> <|4.00|> They woke Jesus up and said to him,<|6.82|> <|7.42|> "'Teacher, do you not care that we are perishing?'<|13.34|> <|13.34|> After calming the storm, rebuking the wind as he had previously rebuked demons,<|21.42|> <|21.88|> he said to them,<|23.12|> <|23.46|> "'Why are you afraid?'<|26.30|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Have you still no faith?<|3.74|> <|5.74|> Why are you afraid?<|8.08|> <|9.48|> Have you still no faith?<|12.10|> <|13.24|> These are pretty painful questions, aren't they?<|17.28|> <|17.86|> It's the heart of this passage, it seems to me, and it makes me wonder why.<|25.20|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Is it Jesus who needed to wake up, or was it the disciples?<|6.92|> <|8.64|> Was it perhaps his followers who needed to wake up and put together all of the clues of what they've witnessed<|18.72|> <|18.72|> so they could truly, truly know who it is that they are following?<|24.26|> <|24.26|> Amen.<|25.10|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> They needed to wake up and pay attention to what's right in front of them.<|5.86|> <|7.62|> My friends, why so often does it take a storm coming into our lives to make us really pay attention?<|18.48|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> I can sympathize with Mark's dense disciples because I know that sometimes I have had to be in the midst of a storm in life to realize who Jesus is.<|13.32|> <|13.98|> Who Jesus is and what power he has to calm the storm, often the one within me.<|22.80|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> who Jesus is and how I have been equipped through my faith to deal with the storm and not be paralyzed by fear.<|11.34|> <|13.32|> Whether our personal struggles, those within us, or those storms outside of us—<|21.60|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> The climate, our economy and job opportunities, homelessness in the city of Denver, any of the rampant isms in our present culture,<|11.76|> <|12.48|> or children being separated from their parents at our southern border.<|18.30|> <|19.58|> We need to wake up.<|22.60|> <|23.48|> We need to pay attention.<|24.88|> <|24.88|> We need to pay attention.<|26.00|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> This life of faith isn't just happening to us.<|6.52|> <|6.98|> It is not a passive existence.<|9.60|> <|10.96|> Jesus invites active participation in his work in the world.<|16.38|> <|17.84|> Wake up.<|18.48|> <|19.92|> Be present to the world and to the work of Jesus in it.<|24.12|> <|24.12|> Amen.<|25.60|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> People often share that their faith grew in a time of trial through the storms.<|9.20|> <|9.20|> They felt closer to God, maybe found God for the first time, or renewed a relationship with God.<|18.90|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> But why do we have to struggle to keep our faith relevant and at the fore when things are going well?<|10.48|> <|12.70|> In the catechumenate class this year, there were several discussions about prayer.<|18.00|> <|18.78|> Why do we pray? How do we pray?<|21.70|> <|21.70|> Why?<|22.30|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Some folks felt unworthy to be able to string together the appropriate words to say before God.<|7.28|> <|8.36|> Some folks wondered about the point of prayer at all if God already knows.<|14.08|> <|15.42|> These were great conversations.<|17.34|> <|18.90|> And it just engendered a wonderful opportunity to share where we feel connected and disconnected.<|24.56|> <|25.44|> And great ideas were bantered about.<|28.20|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> But it didn't really occur to me until working through this text<|5.88|> <|5.88|> that prayer is also fundamental to our ability to stay awake.<|13.70|> <|14.76|> When we are engaging in an active life of prayer,<|18.90|> <|19.86|> praying for our loved ones, our world, our leaders, our common life,<|26.40|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> We are awake to the intersection of ourselves, the needs of others, and God's work in the world.<|9.80|> <|11.46|> We pray for many reasons, but perhaps one of the most important is that it keeps us awake.<|21.28|> <|21.28|> Amen.<|21.50|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> awake and in tune to all of the things within us<|5.20|> <|5.20|> and without us that we need to hold up to God.<|10.08|> <|11.20|> And it can, and at times it absolutely should,<|17.12|> <|18.54|> spur us to action in the midst of the storm.<|21.82|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Amen.<|3.64|><|endoftext|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Thank you.<|29.98|><|endoftext|>

<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> He's taught people through parables, and he's been followed, not just by the disciples, but by great crowds.<|10.24|>
<|11.48|> The disciples accompanied Jesus through all of this, but they still seem to fail to really get who he is.<|24.00|>
<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> It has been pointed out that we should go a little easy on them and not dismiss the severity of this storm.<|7.76|>

<|5.74|> Why are you afraid?<|8.08|>
<|9.48|> Have you still no faith?<|12.10|>
<|13.24|> These are pretty painful questions, aren't they?<|17.28|>
<|17.86|> It's the heart of this passage, it seems to me, and it makes me wonder why.<|25.20|>
<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Is it Jesus who needed to wake up, or was it the disciples?<|6.92|>
<|8.64|> Was it perhaps his followers who needed to wake up and put together all of the clues of what they've witnessed<|18.72|>
<|18.72|> so they could truly, truly know who it is that they are following?<|24.26|>
<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> They needed to wake up and pay attention to what's right in front of them.<|5.86|>
<|7.62|> My friends, why so often does it take a storm coming into our lives to make us really pay attention?<|18.48|>

<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> I can sympathize with Mark's dense disciples because I know that sometimes I have had to be in the midst of a storm in life to realize who Jesus is.<|13.32|>
<|13.98|> Who Jesus is and what power he has to calm the storm, often the one within me.<|22.80|>
<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> who Jesus is and how I have been equipped through my faith to deal with the storm and not be paralyzed by fear.<|11.34|>
<|13.32|> Whether our personal struggles, those within us, or those storms outside of us—<|21.60|>
<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> The climate, our economy and job opportunities, homelessness in the city of Denver, any of the rampant isms in our present culture,<|11.76|>
<|12.48|> or children being separated from their parents at our southern border.<|18.30|>
<|19.58|> We need to wake up.<|22.60|>
<|23.48|> We need to pay attention.<|24.88|>
<|24.88|> We need to pay attention.<|26.00|>
<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> This life of faith isn't just happening to us.<|6.52|>
<|6.98|> It is not a passive existence.<|9.60|>
<|10.96|> Jesus invites active participation in his work in the world.<|16.38|>
<|19.92|> Be present to the world and to the work of Jesus in it.<|24.12|>
<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> People often share that their faith grew in a time of trial through the storms.<|9.20|>
<|9.20|> They felt closer to God, maybe found God for the first time, or renewed a relationship with God.<|18.90|>
<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> But why do we have to struggle to keep our faith relevant and at the fore when things are going well?<|10.48|>
<|12.70|> In the catechumenate class this year, there were several discussions about prayer.<|18.00|>
<|18.78|> Why do we pray? How do we pray?<|21.70|>
<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Some folks felt unworthy to be able to string together the appropriate words to say before God.<|7.28|>
<|8.36|> Some folks wondered about the point of prayer at all if God already knows.<|14.08|>
<|15.42|> These were great conversations.<|17.34|>
<|18.90|> And it just engendered a wonderful opportunity to share where we feel connected and disconnected.<|24.56|>
<|25.44|> And great ideas were bantered about.<|28.20|>
<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> But it didn't really occur to me until working through this text<|5.88|>
<|5.88|> that prayer is also fundamental to our ability to stay awake.<|13.70|>
<|14.76|> When we are engaging in an active life of prayer,<|18.90|>
<|19.86|> praying for our loved ones, our world, our leaders, our common life,<|26.40|>
<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> We are awake to the intersection of ourselves, the needs of others, and God's work in the world.<|9.80|>
<|11.46|> We pray for many reasons, but perhaps one of the most important is that it keeps us awake.<|21.28|>
<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> awake and in tune to all of the things within us<|5.20|>
<|5.20|> and without us that we need to hold up to God.<|10.08|>
<|11.20|> And it can, and at times it absolutely should,<|17.12|>
<|18.54|> spur us to action in the midst of the storm.<|21.82|>
<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Amen.<|3.64|><|endoftext|>
